entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — prospectors and ore-washers: the search for the vein
entries.creator: Georgius Agricola (woodcuts: workshop of Hans Rudolf Manuel Deutsch)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia scan of De Re Metallica (Basel, 1556)]
entries.shelfmark: from Book II (the search for veins)
entries.summary:
A woodcut from the early books of Agricola's *De Re Metallica* showing the prospector's craft: men searching out and washing ore on a wooded hillside cut by a stream. Labelled figures (A, B …) key the workers to Agricola's text, in his characteristic manner of joining picture to instruction.

## Iconography
Across a broken landscape several figures are at work — one striding with a long forked stick (the divining-rod or *virgula furcata* used to dowse for veins), others crouched at the water's edge sorting and washing heaps of ore, a gentleman observer at left. Trees, hillocks and the winding brook set the scene of the open diggings.

## Significance
The search for the vein opens the practical programme of *De Re Metallica*. Agricola describes — and, sceptically, debates — the use of the forked twig to find ore, the lie of the land and the signs of metal in springs and soil. The plate belongs to the foundational work of [[motif:mining|Renaissance mining]] literature, the book that displaced lore with method and that Herbert Hoover and his wife famously translated in 1912.

## For artists & game designers
A prospecting vignette — dowser with forked rod, ore-washers at a stream — a clean reference for a 'survey the land for resources' scene or a divining-rod mechanic.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben, 1556).
Herbert C. Hoover & Lou H. Hoover (trans.), *De Re Metallica* (London, 1912).
Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — the whim (*Göpelwerk*): a man-powered haulage engine (hand-coloured)
entries.creator: Georgius Agricola (woodcuts: workshop of Hans Rudolf Manuel Deutsch)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — hand-coloured German edition (Vom Bergkwerck); Wikimedia scan]
entries.shelfmark: from Book VI (hoisting machines), German ed. 'Vom Bergkwerck'
entries.summary:
A hand-coloured woodcut of a *Göpelwerk* — a great hoisting whim or horse-/man-driven gin used to wind ore and water out of a deep shaft. The German caption *Vom Bergkwerck* and a key of letters (A–H) identify the drum, the cogs and the windlass, with a labourer and a draught-dog beside the machine.

## Iconography
A tall timber A-frame supports a vast horizontal capstan-drum, geared to a smaller toothed wheel that drives the winding cylinder over the shaft mouth. One worker turns or tends the gear below; a red-clad figure and a dog stand on the platform above. The colouring picks out the brown timbers, the green ground and the bright dress of the figures.

## Significance
Agricola's sixth book catalogues the [[motif:machinery|machines]] of the mine — windlasses, whims, rag-and-chain pumps and water-wheels — drawn with an engineer's exactness that makes *De Re Metallica* a primary source for the history of technology. This coloured impression comes from one of the German-language printings ('Vom Bergkwerck'), prized for showing the apparatus in vivid pigment.

## For artists & game designers
A fully realised geared haulage engine in colour — drum, cog-wheels and shaft-head — directly usable as a steampunk-adjacent mine machine or a powered hoist asset.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben, 1556; German ed. *Vom Bergkwerck*).
Herbert C. Hoover & Lou H. Hoover (trans.), *De Re Metallica* (London, 1912).
Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — the smelting works: furnace, bellows and casting under one roof
entries.creator: Georgius Agricola (woodcuts: workshop of Hans Rudolf Manuel Deutsch)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia scan of De Re Metallica (Basel, 1556)]
entries.shelfmark: from the books on smelting (Books VII–IX)
entries.summary:
A woodcut of a smelting house (*Rennwerk* / *Schmelzwerk*) — the building where roasted ore is melted to metal. Under a tiled, open-sided roof a furnace gusts flame at the right while workmen carry charges, tend the fire and work at the casting floor; channels, moulds and tools lie about.

## Iconography
The shed is read in steep perspective: a stone furnace with its fire-arch and a bank of bellows behind, a labourer shouldering a load, a second stooping over the hearth, and at lower right a worker at a low casting-bench beside a barrel and a timber pump-frame. Letter-keys tie each part to the text.

## Significance
Smelting is the climactic operation of [[motif:metallurgy|Agricola's metallurgy]] — the reduction of ore to running metal by fire and forced blast. His furnace-houses, drawn with care to their bellows, fore-hearths and tap-channels, are among the most informative images of sixteenth-century industry, the practical substrate over which the alchemists raised their theory.

## For artists & game designers
A complete smelting-house interior — furnace, bellows, casting bench and barrows — an ideal establishing shot for a foundry level or a metallurgy crafting hub.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben, 1556).
Herbert C. Hoover & Lou H. Hoover (trans.), *De Re Metallica* (London, 1912).
Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — the assayer at the round and rectangular assay furnaces
entries.creator: Georgius Agricola (woodcuts: workshop of Hans Rudolf Manuel Deutsch)
entries.date: 1556 (Basel, Froben; this reproduction after the 1912 Hoover edition)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia scan (Hoover trans., 1912)]
entries.summary:
A pair of woodcuts, captioned in the English (Hoover) edition 'Round assay furnace' and 'Rectangular assay furnace', showing the assayer at work. In each a bearded man sits before a small furnace, watching the test through its little arched muffle-mouth as smoke rises.

## Iconography
In the upper cut the assayer leans toward a tall round furnace, its glowing muffle visible; a low wall and a wooded slope close the background. In the lower cut he sits on a stool at a rectangular furnace, holding tongs to the fire. The two designs document alternative furnace forms for the same delicate operation.

## Significance
Assaying — the small-scale [[motif:fire-test|fire test]] that determines how much gold or silver an ore truly holds — is the subject of Agricola's seventh book and the quantitative heart of the metal trades. [[motif:cupellation|Cupellation]] in the muffle, weighing the bead of precious metal against a known charge, gave the assayer an authority the alchemist envied: a true measure won by fire.

## For artists & game designers
Two clean assay-furnace designs with a seated operator — perfect reference for a precision crafting station or a 'test the ore' minigame.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben, 1556).
Herbert C. Hoover & Lou H. Hoover (trans.), *De Re Metallica* (London, 1912).
Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — the lead hearth (*Bleiofen*): liquation and refining (hand-coloured)
entries.creator: Georgius Agricola (woodcuts: workshop of Hans Rudolf Manuel Deutsch)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — hand-coloured German edition; Wikimedia scan]
entries.shelfmark: from the books on smelting / refining lead and silver
entries.summary:
A hand-coloured woodcut of a lead-furnace and refining hearth (*Bleiofen*), the workplace where lead is smelted and silver parted from it. Under a tiled roof and against a brick furnace-wall several workmen ply ladles and rakes over shallow hearths, their stations keyed by letters (A–M).

## Iconography
The brick masonry of the works fills the upper register, a fire blazing in an arched opening at the right. Below, hooded labourers stoop over round and oval hearths, skimming, raking and pouring; buckets and tools stand ready. The colouring warms the brick, the flames and the workers' dress.

## Significance
The parting of [[motif:metallurgy|silver from lead]] — by liquation and by [[motif:cupellation|cupellation]] on the great refining hearth — was among the most valuable operations of the age, the engine of the Saxon and Harz silver economies Agricola served as town physician. The coloured German impression renders the hearth-work with unusual vividness.

## For artists & game designers
A coloured refining-hearth scene with multiple work-stations — ready material for a smelting-line interface or a busy foundry-floor backdrop.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben, 1556; German ed. *Vom Bergkwerck*).
Herbert C. Hoover & Lou H. Hoover (trans.), *De Re Metallica* (London, 1912).
Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — the mine truck (Book VI): construction of the *Hund*
entries.creator: Georgius Agricola (woodcuts: workshop of Hans Rudolf Manuel Deutsch)
entries.date: 1556 (Basel, Froben; this reproduction after the 1912 Hoover edition)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia scan (Hoover trans., 1912)]
entries.summary:
A woodcut from Book VI, headed 'BOOK VI' in the English edition, giving an exploded study of the mine truck (the *Hund*) used to carry ore along the galleries. A lettered key names every part — the iron bands and straps, the iron axle, the wooden rollers, the small iron keys and the large blunt pin — and the same truck is shown upright and turned upside down.

## Iconography
The loaded box-truck sits on its little wheels by a tree at upper right; below, the body is drawn again inverted to expose the rollers and ironwork, with the loose components (axle, pin, keys, rollers) laid out separately and labelled A–G. It is a parts-diagram in the modern sense.

## Significance
Agricola's exploded mechanical drawings are a landmark in technical illustration: he shows not only the machine in use but its components disassembled and named, so that the reader could build one. The ore-truck running in a wooden guide-track is an ancestor of the railway, and Book VI's catalogue of [[motif:machinery|mine machinery]] remains a key source for the history of engineering.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic exploded-view diagram of an ore cart — usable as a craftable-vehicle blueprint, an inventory schematic, or a tutorial 'assemble the parts' asset.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben, 1556).

entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — letterpress title page with the Froben device (1556)
entries.creator: Georgius Agricola (printer: Hieronymus Froben & Nicolaus Episcopius)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia scan of De Re Metallica (Basel, 1556)]
entries.summary:
The letterpress title page of the first edition of *De Re Metallica* (Basel, 1556): *GEORGII AGRICOLAE DE RE METALLICA LIBRI XII … BASILEAE M D LVI*. Recorded honestly as a title page rather than as one of the technical plates. At its centre stands the printer's device of the house of Froben.

## Iconography
Roman and italic type announce the twelve books on mining and metallurgy and the appended *De Animantibus Subterraneis* ('On Subterranean Creatures'). Between the title and the imprint sits the famous Froben mark — a caduceus, twin serpents twined about a staff and surmounted by a dove, flanked by the legend FRO-BEN — and at the foot, *Cum Privilegio Imperatoris in annos V. & Galliarum Regis ad Sexennium.*

## Significance
The Froben caduceus marks one of the great scholarly presses of the Renaissance — the house of Erasmus's printer — and lends Agricola's metallurgy the authority of humanist Basel. The book appeared in 1556, the year after the author's death, and became the standard treatise on [[motif:mining|mining]] for two centuries.

## For artists & game designers
An authentic Renaissance title page with a serpent-and-staff printer's mark — a strong reference for a book prop, a guild seal, or an alchemical-press device.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben, 1556).
Herbert C. Hoover & Lou H. Hoover (trans.), *De Re Metallica* (London, 1912).
Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — letterpress title page with the Froben device (1561 issue)
entries.creator: Georgius Agricola (printer: house of Froben)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia scan of De Re Metallica (Basel, Froben)]
entries.summary:
The letterpress title page of a later Froben issue of *De Re Metallica* — *GEORGII AGRICOLAE DE RE METALLICA LIBRI XII … BASILEAE M D LXI* — again with the appended *De Animantibus Subterraneis Liber*, here noted as 'recognised by the author' and reset. Recorded as a title page rather than a technical plate.

## Iconography
The layout repeats that of the first edition: the twelve-book title and its claims of full Latin and German nomenclature for offices, instruments and machines, the Froben caduceus device (twin serpents on a staff crowned by a dove, FRO-BEN at either side) at centre, and the imperial-and-royal privilege at the foot. The dated imprint (MDLXI) distinguishes this from the 1556 *editio princeps*.

## Significance
The repeated printings under the Froben mark attest to the enduring demand for Agricola's treatise on [[motif:mining|mining and metallurgy]]. The *De Animantibus Subterraneis* bound with it — on the creatures, real and fabled, of the underground — links the sober technical work to the period's lore of mine-spirits and gnomes.

## For artists & game designers
A second, cleanly framed Renaissance title page with the serpent-caduceus device — useful as an alternate book-cover plate or printer's-mark reference.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben; this issue 1561).
Herbert C. Hoover & Lou H. Hoover (trans.), *De Re Metallica* (London, 1912).
Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — modern Ukrainian edition cover (Books I–VI)
entries.creator: after Georgius Agricola (modern Ukrainian translation; cover designer unknown)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — modern Ukrainian edition (Про гірничу справу); Wikimedia scan]
entries.rights: Public domain (cover reproduces public-domain motifs; image via Wikimedia).
entries.summary:
The printed cover of a modern Ukrainian translation of Agricola — *Георгій Агрікола, Про гірничу справу XII книг (Книги I–VI)* ('Georgius Agricola, On Mining, in twelve books — Books I–VI'). Recorded honestly as a modern book cover, not an original sixteenth-century plate.

## Iconography
Within an ornate painted architectural frame — flanking putti, one bearing a pick, scrollwork and a winged cartouche above — an oval vignette shows a rocky, sunlit landscape with small figures at the diggings; a lower panel depicts an interior with seated figures. The Cyrillic title is set over the oval.

## Significance
The cover testifies to the long afterlife of *De Re Metallica*: still translated and reissued, here into Ukrainian, four and a half centuries after its first appearance. Its decorative frame consciously evokes the Renaissance manner, though the design is a modern confection rather than an Agricola original.

## For artists & game designers
A modern pastiche of a Renaissance title border — useful as an example of revival design, or a frame template, but not as a primary historical source.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben, 1556); modern Ukrainian trans. *Про гірничу справу*.
Herbert C. Hoover & Lou H. Hoover (trans.), *De Re Metallica* (London, 1912).
Project Gutenberg / Wikimedia Commons

entries.title: *De Re Metallica* — modern Ukrainian edition cover (Books VII–XII)
entries.creator: after Georgius Agricola (modern Ukrainian translation; cover designer unknown)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — modern Ukrainian edition (Про гірничу справу); Wikimedia scan]
entries.rights: Public domain (cover reproduces public-domain motifs; image via Wikimedia).
entries.summary:
The companion volume cover of the modern Ukrainian Agricola — *Георгій Агрікола, Про гірничу справу XII книг (Книги VII–XII)* ('On Mining, in twelve books — Books VII–XII'). Recorded honestly as a modern book cover rather than a period plate.

## Iconography
The design mirrors its companion: a richly coloured architectural frame with flanking putti (one shouldering a pick), scrolled volutes and a winged crest, enclosing an oval vignette of a craggy landscape under a radiant sun with miners at work, and a figured interior panel below. The Cyrillic title sits across the oval.

## Significance
The two-volume set divides Agricola's twelve books between Books I–VI (prospecting, surveying, machines) and Books VII–XII (assaying, smelting, refining, salts and the subterranean creatures). The cover again demonstrates the continuing [[motif:reception|reception]] of *De Re Metallica* as a living technical classic well into the present.

## For artists & game designers
A matching modern revival cover — handy as a paired set-design reference or an example of neo-Renaissance frame styling; not a primary historical source.

citations.text: Georgius Agricola, *De Re Metallica* (Basel: Froben, 1556); modern Ukrainian trans. *Про гірничу справу*.
